transcript.text: We all know that Claude here is able to
take any ideas and is able to generate
it, no problem. But, do we really trust
the code that Claude write? Do we have
the right automation testing? Do we have
the right framework in place? Is it able
to have a consistent output? And most
importantly, what about security? And
maybe the time that it takes for Claude
here to fix the issue that it wrote, it
take longer compared to just writing the
code ourself. And the solution to this
from the community is there are three
popular framework that the community
built. One is GSD, which currently has
40 thousand stars on GitHub. I actually
made a video on my channel on how to use
GSD. And then there's also GStack, which
is built by Gary Tang, who is the CEO of
Y Combinator. And then there's also the
plugin for Superpowers, which is a
genetic skill framework that I made a
video on on my channel as well. And
based on this chart, you can see that
there are three frameworks. And you
might be asking, "Well, are they trying
to solve the same thing?" Well, the
answer is no. And all three frameworks
here are trying to solve a particular
problem in this process. And that's why
in this video, I'm going to show you
exactly what each of those framework
does, how are they different, and most
importantly, how can we be able to use
that to improve accuracy for building
applications using Claude. Now, before
we continue, I recently launched our
school community where I help you to
master AI agents, automations, and so
much more. And that's all coming from
someone who used to work as a senior AI
software engineer at companies like
Amazon and Microsoft. And in this
community, you're going to get over 100
plus video materials like templates and
workflows that I personally built and
sold over 100 plus times. On top of
that, you're also going to get access to
our weekly live calls. And just give you
an idea, this week we're actually
running a Claude masterclass where we're
going to dive into how to improve
Claude's accuracy when we're going to
use it to building applications. Plus,
you're also going to get full community
support where you're going to get a
chance to ask questions and get direct
answers back. So, if you're ready to
level up, make sure you jump right in
and I'll see you in the community. Now,
first we're going to take a look at is
Superpowers. Basically, what it
essentially does here is that having
Claude here to follow a strict framework
or a software development methodology
here to complete a task step-by-step.
So, instead of having Claude here to
give it instructions, maybe have it to,
you know, code randomly or maybe like
not really consistent every single time,
we're going to have a basically like a
framework. So, it's going to first
asking some clarification questions,
right? Try to clarify the intent. And
then it's going to confirm on exactly is
this what you were trying to build,
right? It's going to create a spec. Then
it's going to create a plan on exactly
how we're going to do this, like the
entire implementation plan. And
furthermore, what's really unique about
this framework here is that it's also
following the test driven approach.
Which essentially what it does here is
that it's going to first try to set the
rules on exactly how the software here
is going to behave, right? It's going to
first try to create a test before it's
going to try to write the code, try to
do the implementation. We're going to
guarantee that agent here is going to
write code because we already have the
automation testing in place. And then
after the code is written and the test
is passed, then it's going to do some
refactoring until we have nothing to
refactor anymore, right? Until the code
here is more scalable and also the
application here behave as what we
expected in the tests. And that's
basically what Superpowers here in a
nutshell. Now, if Superpowers here is
really try to set up the agenda
framework on how AI agent here do
things, GSD here is trying to solve the
context rot. And basically, what context
rot means is that if you were to have a
conversation with Claude,
maybe the first 20% is good, right? But
the time when you get to like 40, 60,
80%, and then the accuracy here is
starting to drop dramatically. And it's
the same for any other large language
models. And what GSD believe in is that
if we were to have the context window
always stay below 50%, we're going to
get the best accuracy from large
language model all the time. And you can
see here that it's going to break
different phases here into different
stages. And for each stage here, it's
going to trigger different sub agents
here to execute it. And the way how
different stages here keep it consistent
on which stage we're in, it uses the MD
file to save in our local disk to keep
track with process. And basically, for
example, if session one is complete,
it's going to save the state here inside
of the MD file in our local machine. And
then for the next session after Claude
is going to spin up, it's going to read
through the state that we put in our
local disk and it's going to continue
the task from there. And like I said,
both of Superpowers here and GSD all
uses sub agents for different stage.
Like for example, the phase one here,
phase one right here, is all doing
research. Yes, they're going to spin up
different sub agents to do this. But the
main difference here is that yes,
Superpowers here has its own framework,
but the problem here is that they have a
mega orchestrator that doing everything.
Like for example, this is a phase one,
this is phase two, this is phase three,
phase four. They have different sub
agents spinning up for each phases. But
the context for the orchestrator here
never change, right? Is we're using the
same orchestrator throughout the entire
conversation. But the difference for GSD
here, yes, the GSD is using sub agents
to do all different phases. But for each
phase here, we are actually switching
different orchestrators. So, for phase
one, we have orchestrator one. For phase
two, we have orchestrator two, right?
So, after orchestrator two is done, it's
going to save the states in our local
disk. Then it's going to spin up the
phase three sub agents here and also the
orchestrator to do the task. So, fresh
starts after every phase, memory are
saved in a disk. And for Superpowers
here, you can see it's just one
conversation, always awake. And here you
can see this is another illustration
here to put it simply. Like I said,
Superpowers here we have brainstorm,
plan, execution. We are all done using
this one orchestrator. And this
orchestrator here is going to spin up
different sub agents here to complete
this. But for GSD, we're going to have
one orchestrator for phase one, one
orchestrator for phase two, one
orchestrator for phase three. This way,
the orchestrator here is always going to
be under 50% for the context window. So,
you can see that both of them are trying
to solve different problem. One is try
to set the standards on exactly how AI
agent here do things, right? Brainstorm,
plan, execute. And the other one is try
to making sure that every agent here is
staying under 50% for the context
window, even including the orchestrator
themself. And lastly, we also have
GStack. And what GStack mission
statement here is basically try to break
the single agent here into different
specialist role, right? So, instead of
having one general agent do everything,
we're going to break it down into
different roles. For example, CEO role,
maybe a engineer role, designer role, or
maybe also a release engineer role,
right? There could be many roles by the
time you watch this video. So, for
example, whenever we come to the stage
of planning, maybe we can be able to
trigger the CEO lens here to review the
things. Or whenever we try to do the
building, it's going to trigger the
engineering manager, right? So, it's
going to go through this process here by
different stages. And for each stage
here, it's going to trigger a specialist
agent. And you might be wondering,
"Well, how is it different compared to
Superpowers stages?" Right? Superpowers
also has brainstorming, writing plans,
all those kind of things. The key
difference here is that Superpowers is
only act as a discipline engineer, which
basically means that it's like a tech
lead following these software
development methodologies, but it
doesn't switch to different lenses on
like seeing different roles perspective
like QA, you know, actually be able to
open the browser here, creating a test
plan. Also, it doesn't have the ability
here to like plan as a CEO, right? So,
these are all different specialist
agents that GStack here is also
operating differently compared to
Superpowers. And lastly, you might be
wondering, "Well, are these just role
prompts that we just basically tell
Claude, hey, I want you to behave a
certain way?" Well, the answer is it's
actually not because there's actually a
five layers, you can see here, of
mechanism on how it actually behave for
each roles that we have seen here to
making sure that each role here doesn't
break characters. And starting the first
one here is the role focus. And
basically, what it does here is that
it's going to put on the blenders. So,
whenever it's going to put on this role
like for example, QA lead, it's only
going to focus on what QA have to focus
on, right? For example, the user flow,
the bug report, testing checklist, or
what's the result look like, right? It's
going to look through that. But it
doesn't look through any other things
that's not part of their responsibility.
For example, the code style, data
schema, is other specialist agent's job.
And this agent here is only focusing on
his job responsibility. And then the
second layer here is data flow. And
basically, the way how it works for
these agents or these specialist agents
here, you can see is that their work is
built on top of the previous stage. For
example, let's say this is the reviewer
agent. Reviewer agent here say that,
"Okay, looks good. Uh please help me to
QA this work helping me to test this."
Then the QA lead here is going to take
this response, which is how the data is
actually flowing. And then we also have
quality control, which is a checklist on
exactly what everyone can see. For
example, for CEO, yes, it has done the
review. Engineer manager has done the
review as well, right? So, we have a
checklist on exactly what roles has been
processed based on a certain project
we're currently doing. And then the
fourth layer here is basically boil the
lake. What it means to here is that
trying to finish what you can do
perfectly and don't start doing anything
that you cannot do perfectly. For
example, it's easier to boil a small
lake because it's very small, right? You
can be able to do this perfectly fine.
But if it's like outside of your
responsibility, if it's something that
you cannot be able to do perfectly, for
example, you cannot be able to boil a
ocean because it's huge, right? So, if
you cannot do it, then don't do it,
right? So, what we want to do here is
that we want to do something that you
can be able to do 100%. And so so you
can see here that's exactly what we're
trying to set in the layer four. And the
layer five here is really just try to
keep things simple. Like instead of
having so many things thrown at you,
it's going to take it slow down and try
to summarize everything. Like it's going
to boil it down into these three things.
Like here's what I found, why it
matters, and what to do next. It's going
to give you a direct answer, explain to
you like a 16-year-old, and that's it.
And lastly, just to conclude everything
for all three frameworks, you can see
that for Superpowers here is helping you
to constrain the process, making sure
that AI agent here is following the test
driven development framework here,
making sure that we set the expectation
before we going to do the execution. GSD
here is making sure that we constrain
the environment, making sure that every
session that we have here is below 50%
for the context window so that we ensure
AI agent here is able to output with,
you know, high accuracy, right? And then
GStack here is basically constrained
with perspective. Means that we're going
to focusing on different lenses, right?
Having different persona, different
specialist reviewing different stages of
the development process, rather than
just having one general agent that does
everything. So, then you might be
asking, "Well, every framework here is
trying to solve a particular problem. Is
it possible if we can be able to combine
them all into a power stack? And the
answer here is yes, we can definitely do
that. You can see here we can combine
the power of G stack, which is really
good at, you know, executive team. GSD
here is really good at project manager.
Superpowers here is really good as being
a senior engineer, right? So, what we
can do here is that for planning, for
strategic planning, like let's say we
want to have a big idea or vague idea, G
stack here has different persona. It's
going to help you to, like for example,
these different skills here, like CEO,
engineer manager here, is going to help
you to craft a overall architecture. Is
this project idea valid, right? It's
going to help you to refine that project
idea a bit more. And then when it comes
to taking that project idea or taking
that project plan into a execution, like
how we can be able to break it down into
different task or different milestones,
it's really good at using GSD because
GSD here is really thinking about
context protection, making sure that AI
agent here is going to be stay under 50%
for the context window. And they're
going to do this by calculating the
projects and breaking that project into
different milestones so that each
milestone here can be done under, you
know, 50% for using just one
orchestrator agents. And then finally,
when it comes to execution, superpower
here is going to be really good because
it follow the test driven development.
So, what we can do here is that we can
trigger those skills and be able to
implement those features by forcing AI
here to write the test before it does
the coding work. And finally, we can
have G stack here to do the QA polishing
work to making sure that it's able to
use playwright here to actually build
click using the UI here to test it. So,
eventually here you can see that's
exactly how each one of them works. And
what we can do here is that we can be
able to piece them together into our
overall development workflows. For
example, G stack is really good at
planning, QA. Maybe other frameworks are
not really good at that. We can use that
for that particular job. If it's like
project management, like breaking the
the entire project into different
milestones, then we're going to do it
with GSD. And making sure that each
milestone here is going to take less
than 50% of the context when the
superpower agent here is going to do it.
Right? And then when we trigger the
superpower agents, we're going to have
that agent here is focusing on just
writing code, making sure that we follow
the strict testing, having parallel sub
agents here going to run at the same
time. And that's exactly how we can do
this. We can be able to take the best of
all three frameworks to making sure that
everything is accurate and stable, and
also consistent throughout the entire
development process. So, with that being
said, if you do find value in this
video, please make sure to like this
video, and be sure to check out this
playlist right here on all these
spectrum development frameworks that I
have talked about on this channel. And
of course, if you do find value in this
video, please make sure to like this
video, consider subscribing more content
like this. But with that being said,
I'll see you in the next video.
